title: The footprints of every building in NYC
excerpt: The landscape of NYC changes so much that the New York City Office of Technology and Innovation updates a dataset of building footprint shapes weekly.
content_html:
The landscape of NYC changes so much that the New York City Office of Technology and Innovation updates a dataset of building footprint shapes weekly.

I can't believe this is the first time I am writing about an NYC dataset.

The scale of New York City can be overwhelming. Over 8.5 million people are estimated to live in the five boroughs of NYC, squished into 300 square miles, making it the most densely populated major city in the US.

All of those people live and work in buildings of vastly different sizes and shapes — soaring skyscrapers, 200-year-old brownstones, single-story houses, and everything in between. As of July 13, 2026, there are 1,082,974 buildings that New York City tracks. Anyone who has spent any amount of time in NYC knows that the city is under a permanent state of construction, and demolition — massive new buildings just pop up out of nowhere all the time. 

The pace of change is so rapid in fact, that the New York City Office of Technology and Innovation (OTI) publishes weekly updates to one of its most fascinating datasets – the footprints of every building in New York City. The OTI says that its accurate basemap provides “the foundation upon which virtually all other geospatial data within New York City government is registered.” 

What’s included in this data?

Like I said — this dataset has all buildings in NYC. The OTI says it counts every building greater than 400 square feet, and taller than 12 feet. The items that aren’t counted: temporary trailers, tents, movable jet bridges, awnings, scaffolds, sidewalk sheds, and any backyard storage sheds.  

The dataset contains classifications for the structures. The vast majority are listed as buildings, but other types are in there as well. For example, one-fifth of the structures in the data are garages.  

99% of buildings in the dataset have a construction date, so we can get a glimpse of when the busiest times for construction were (although this dataset only contains buildings that are still around today). Nearly a quarter of all existing New York City buildings were constructed in the 1920s. 

The height of the buildings in the dataset doesn't tell the full story of the city’s tallest buildings you might be familiar with. One World Trade Center’s enormous spire brought the total height of the building to a symbolic 1,776 feet. But in the data, the height of the roof is reported as 1,408 feet. The Council on Vertical Urbanism’s Skyscraper Center lists One World Trade’s “occupied” height at 1,268 feet. The discrepancy is likely due to what the photogrammetry defines as the roof. 

How is it collected?

It may seem to be practically impossible to precisely measure so many buildings that change so fast. If you are picturing an army of city workers with tape measures, you have the wrong idea. 

The vast majority — about 93% — of the measurements of the city’s buildings are generated by photogrammetry (deriving 3D geometry from a series of photographs). The OTI’s data dictionary for the data says that annually captured oblique aerial imagery from Eagleview and ground-level panoramic photography from Cyclomedia are used to generate the measurements of the buildings, along with Department of Building research and other city records. The remainder of the building data is entered manually using other less accurate methods. 

According to the OTI, 95% of the footprint data is accurate to plus or minus two feet.

History

There’s a lot of history in this data. The oldest building still standing today in NYC is in there — the Pieter Claesen Wyckoff House in East Flatbush, Brooklyn, which was built in 1652. 

NYC has been using aerial photography for over 100 years to catalog and measure the city. Thanks to some superb data preservation, you can explore some amazing aerial photography data going back to 1924.

And what about all of those buildings from the past are no longer with us? Don’t worry – there’s a dataset for that. NYC’s “Building Historic” dataset contains “buildings that have been demolished or significantly altered.” This dataset follows pretty much the same format as the current building footprints, but with an additional field for construction year or alteration year. 

Captured in this set of ghost buildings you will find whole city blocks that were stamped out of existence, like the one that contained my old Prospect Heights, Brooklyn neighborhood dive bar Freddie's, which was erased to build the Barclays Center and the Atlantic Yards project. 

Looking at the buildings that were demolished that also had a construction date, we can calculate the lifespan of the buildings that are gone. 14 of the demolished buildings were over 200 years old. You can explore these missing buildings in an interactive map here.

Layers upon layers of spatial data

I loaded up the footprints shapefile into QGIS and created these visualizations that use a color scale to indicate elevation (the building roof). Brighter colors equals higher elevation. I also imported a full-city digital surface model (DSM), which is generated by LiDAR scans gathered by low-altitude airplane flights over the city.

The DSM captures hills, trees, bridges (parts of them), train tracks, and even airplanes on the tarmac at JFK and LaGuardia. Combining this layer with the crisp building outlines makes for a vibrant map of the many elements of the physical city that are captured in this fascinating public data.

👨🏻‍💻

Nerdbox
For this post, I downloaded shapefiles, and DSM files from NYC's fantastic, extensive Open Data portal. I loaded them into QGIS, and combined the building footprint shapefiles with the DSM file, which I applied a "singleband pseudocolor" color scale to visualize elevation.

🙏🏻

blocks.html_en: History

sentences.en: History
sentences.zh: 历史

sentences.zh: 这些数据中有很多历史。

sentences.en: Thanks to some superb data preservation, you can explore some amazing aerial photography data going back to 1924.
sentences.zh: 得益于出色的数据保存，您可以探索一些可追溯到 1924 年的令人惊叹的航空摄影数据。

blocks.html_en: And what about all of those buildings from the past are no longer with us? Don’t worry – there’s a dataset for that. NYC’s “Building Historic” dataset contains “buildings that have been demolished or significantly altered.” This dataset follows pretty much the same format as the current building footprints, but with an additional field for construction year or alteration year.

sentences.en: And what about all of those buildings from the past are no longer with us?
sentences.zh: 那么那些过去的建筑现在已经不在我们身边了呢？

sentences.en: Don’t worry – there’s a dataset for that.
sentences.zh: 别担心——有一个数据集。

sentences.en: NYC’s “Building Historic” dataset contains “buildings that have been demolished or significantly altered.”
sentences.zh: 纽约市的“历史建筑”数据集包含“已被拆除或发生重大改变的建筑”。

sentences.en: This dataset follows pretty much the same format as the current building footprints, but with an additional field for construction year or alteration year.
sentences.zh: 该数据集遵循与当前建筑物占地面积几乎相同的格式，但具有用于建造年份或改造年份的附加字段。

sentences.en: Left to right: Governor's Island; Mill Basin, Brooklyn; Midtown Manhattan.
sentences.zh: 从左到右：总督岛；米尔盆地，布鲁克林；曼哈顿中城。

sentences.en: Visualization: Jon Keegan / Beautiful Public Data.
sentences.zh: 可视化：Jon Keegan / 美丽的公共数据。

sentences.zh: 数据：纽约市技术与创新办公室

blocks.html_en: Captured in this set of ghost buildings you will find whole city blocks that were stamped out of existence, like the one that contained my old Prospect Heights, Brooklyn neighborhood dive bar Freddie's, which was erased to build the Barclays Center and the Atlantic Yards project.

sentences.en: Captured in this set of ghost buildings you will find whole city blocks that were stamped out of existence, like the one that contained my old Prospect Heights, Brooklyn neighborhood dive bar Freddie's, which was erased to build the Barclays Center and the Atlantic Yards project.
sentences.zh: 在这组幽灵建筑中，你会发现整个城市街区都被抹去了，就像我以前在布鲁克林附近的 Prospect Heights 的潜水酒吧 Freddie's 所在的街区一样，它被删除以建造巴克莱中心和大西洋庭院项目。

sentences.en: Some of the buildings that don't exist anymore from the NYC "Building Historic" dataset, including the original Prospect Heights location for Freddie's Bar 🥲.
sentences.zh: 纽约市“建筑历史”数据集中不再存在的一些建筑物，包括 Freddie's Bar 🥲 在 Prospect Heights 的原始位置。

blocks.html_en: Looking at the buildings that were demolished that also had a construction date, we can calculate the lifespan of the buildings that are gone. 14 of the demolished buildings were over 200 years old. You can explore these missing buildings in an interactive map here.

sentences.en: Looking at the buildings that were demolished that also had a construction date, we can calculate the lifespan of the buildings that are gone.
sentences.zh: 看看那些被拆除的建筑物，也有建造日期，我们就可以计算出那些已经消失的建筑物的寿命。

sentences.en: 14 of the demolished buildings were over 200 years old.
sentences.zh: 被拆除的建筑中有14栋的历史超过200年。

sentences.en: You can explore these missing buildings in an interactive map here.
sentences.zh: 您可以在此处的交互式地图中探索这些缺失的建筑物。

blocks.html_en: Layers upon layers of spatial data

sentences.en: Layers upon layers of spatial data
sentences.zh: 层层空间数据

blocks.html_en: I loaded up the footprints shapefile into QGIS and created these visualizations that use a color scale to indicate elevation (the building roof). Brighter colors equals higher elevation. I also imported a full-city digital surface model (DSM), which is generated by LiDAR scans gathered by low-altitude airplane flights over the city.

sentences.en: I loaded up the footprints shapefile into QGIS and created these visualizations that use a color scale to indicate elevation (the building roof).
sentences.zh: 我将足迹形状文件加载到 QGIS 中，并创建了这些可视化效果，使用色标来指示标高（建筑物屋顶）。

sentences.en: Brighter colors equals higher elevation.
sentences.zh: 更明亮的颜色等于更高的海拔。

sentences.en: I also imported a full-city digital surface model (DSM), which is generated by LiDAR scans gathered by low-altitude airplane flights over the city.
sentences.zh: 我还导入了全城市数字表面模型 (DSM)，该模型是由低空飞机在城市上空飞行收集的 LiDAR 扫描生成的。

blocks.html_en: How LiDAR measures the toll of climate disasters

sentences.en: How LiDAR measures the toll of climate disasters
sentences.zh: 激光雷达如何测量气候灾害造成的损失

blocks.html_en: Comparing LiDAR data from before and after the January 2025 fires in Los Angeles reveals the scale of devastation in ways satellite imagery can’t match.

sentences.en: Comparing LiDAR data from before and after the January 2025 fires in Los Angeles reveals the scale of devastation in ways satellite imagery can’t match.
sentences.zh: 比较 2025 年 1 月洛杉矶火灾前后的激光雷达数据，揭示了卫星图像无法比拟的破坏规模。

sentences.en: Beautiful Public DataJon Keegan
sentences.zh: 美丽的公共数据Jon Keegan

sentences.en: You can see cruise ships captured in this 2017 digital surface model of Manhattan's west side.
sentences.zh: 您可以看到在这张 2017 年曼哈顿西区数字表面模型中捕捉到的游轮。

sentences.en: Visualization: Jon Keegan / Beautiful Public Data.
sentences.zh: 可视化：Jon Keegan / 美丽的公共数据。

sentences.zh: 数据：纽约市技术与创新办公室

blocks.html_en: The DSM captures hills, trees, bridges (parts of them), train tracks, and even airplanes on the tarmac at JFK and LaGuardia. Combining this layer with the crisp building outlines makes for a vibrant map of the many elements of the physical city that are captured in this fascinating public data.

sentences.en: The DSM captures hills, trees, bridges (parts of them), train tracks, and even airplanes on the tarmac at JFK and LaGuardia.
sentences.zh: DSM 可以捕捉山丘、​​树木、桥梁（部分）、火车轨道，甚至肯尼迪机场和拉瓜迪亚机场停机坪上的飞机。

sentences.en: Combining this layer with the crisp building outlines makes for a vibrant map of the many elements of the physical city that are captured in this fascinating public data.
sentences.zh: 将这一层与清晰的建筑轮廓相结合，形成了一幅充满活力的地图，其中包含了这个令人着迷的公共数据中捕获的物理城市的许多元素。

sentences.en: Planes and jetways lined up at JFK.
sentences.zh: 飞机和登机口在肯尼迪国际机场排队。

sentences.en: The digital surface model from 2017 shows different building outlines from 2026's building footprints.
sentences.zh: 2017 年的数字表面模型显示了与 2026 年建筑足迹不同的建筑轮廓。

sentences.en: Visualization: Jon Keegan / Beautiful Public Data.
sentences.zh: 可视化：Jon Keegan / 美丽的公共数据。

sentences.en: Data: New York City Office of Technology and Innovation
sentences.zh: 数据：纽约市技术与创新办公室

blocks.html_en:
Nerdbox
For this post, I downloaded shapefiles, and DSM files from NYC's fantastic, extensive Open Data portal. I loaded them into QGIS, and combined the building footprint shapefiles with the DSM file, which I applied a "singleband pseudocolor" color scale to visualize elevation.

sentences.en: NerdboxFor this post, I downloaded shapefiles, and DSM files from NYC's fantastic, extensive Open Data portal.
sentences.zh: Nerdbox 对于这篇文章，我从纽约市精彩而广泛的开放数据门户下载了 shapefile 和 DSM 文件。

sentences.en: I loaded them into QGIS, and combined the building footprint shapefiles with the DSM file, which I applied a "singleband pseudocolor" color scale to visualize elevation.
sentences.zh: 我将它们加载到 QGIS 中，并将建筑占地面积形状文件与 DSM 文件结合起来，并应用“单带伪彩色”色标来可视化高程。

content_html_zh:
纽约市的景观变化如此之大，以至于纽约市技术与创新办公室每周都会更新建筑足迹形状的数据集。

我不敢相信这是我第一次撰写有关纽约数据集的文章。

纽约市的规模可能是巨大的。 据估计，超过 850 万人居住在纽约市的五个行政区，这五个行政区被压缩成 300 平方英里，使其成为美国人口最稠密的主要城市。

所有这些人都在大小和形状截然不同的建筑中生活和工作——高耸的摩天大楼、有 200 年历史的褐砂石建筑、单层房屋，以及介于两者之间的一切。 截至 2026 年 7 月 13 日，纽约市追踪了 1,082,974 栋建筑物。 任何在纽约呆过一段时间的人都知道，这座城市正处于永久的建设和拆除状态——大量的新建筑总是突然冒出来。

事实上，变化的速度是如此之快，以至于纽约市技术与创新办公室 (OTI) 每周都会发布其最引人入胜的数据集之一的更新——纽约市每栋建筑的足迹。 OTI 表示，其准确的底图提供了“纽约市政府内几乎所有其他地理空间数据注册的基础”。

这些数据包含什么？

就像我说的——这个数据集包含纽约市的所有建筑物。 OTI 表示，它统计了所有面积超过 400 平方英尺、高度超过 12 英尺的建筑物。 不计算在内的物品：临时拖车、帐篷、可移动登机桥、遮阳篷、脚手架、人行道棚和任何后院储藏棚。

数据集包含结构的分类。 绝大多数被列为建筑物，但也有其他类型。 例如，数据中五分之一的建筑物是车库。

数据集中 99% 的建筑物都有施工日期，因此我们可以了解施工最繁忙的时间是什么时候（尽管该数据集仅包含今天仍然存在的建筑物）。 纽约市现有建筑中近四分之一建于 20 世纪 20 年代。

数据集中建筑物的高度并不能说明您可能熟悉的城市最高建筑物的全部情况。 世贸中心一号大楼巨大的尖顶使建筑总高度达到象征性的 1,776 英尺。 但在数据中，屋顶的高度被报道为 1,408 英尺。 垂直城市主义委员会的摩天大楼中心列出世贸一号大楼的“占用”高度为 1,268 英尺。 这种差异可能是由于摄影测量对屋顶的定义造成的。

它是如何收集的？

精确测量如此多变化如此之快的建筑物似乎实际上是不可能的。 如果您想象的是一群拿着卷尺的城市工人，那么您的想法就错了。

城市建筑的绝大多数（约 93%）测量结果是通过摄影测量（从一系列照片中得出 3D 几何图形）生成的。 OTI 的数据字典称，每年从 Eagleview 捕获的倾斜航空图像和 Cyclomedia 的地面全景摄影以及建筑研究部和其他城市记录用于生成建筑物的测量结果。 其余的建筑数据是使用其他不太准确的方法手动输入的。

根据 OTI 的说法，95% 的足迹数据精确到正负两英尺。

历史

这些数据中有很多历史。 纽约市现存最古老的建筑就在那里——位于布鲁克林东弗拉特布什的彼得·克莱森·威科夫故居 (Pieter Claesen Wyckoff House)，建于 1652 年。

纽约市使用航空摄影来编目和测量这座城市已有 100 多年的历史。 得益于出色的数据保存，您可以探索一些可追溯到 1924 年的令人惊叹的航空摄影数据。

那么那些过去的建筑现在已经不在我们身边了呢？ 别担心——有一个数据集。 纽约市的“历史建筑”数据集包含“已被拆除或发生重大改变的建筑”。 该数据集遵循与当前建筑物占地面积几乎相同的格式，但具有用于建造年份或改造年份的附加字段。

在这组幽灵建筑中，你会发现整个城市街区都被抹去了，就像我以前在布鲁克林附近的 Prospect Heights 的潜水酒吧 Freddie's 所在的街区一样，它被删除以建造巴克莱中心和大西洋庭院项目。

看看那些被拆除的建筑物，也有建造日期，我们就可以计算出那些已经消失的建筑物的寿命。 被拆除的建筑中有14栋的历史超过200年。 您可以在此处的交互式地图中探索这些缺失的建筑物。

层层空间数据

我将足迹形状文件加载到 QGIS 中，并创建了这些可视化效果，使用色标来指示标高（建筑物屋顶）。 更明亮的颜色等于更高的海拔。 我还导入了全城市数字表面模型 (DSM)，该模型是由低空飞机在城市上空飞行收集的 LiDAR 扫描生成的。

DSM 可以捕捉山丘、​​树木、桥梁（部分）、火车轨道，甚至肯尼迪机场和拉瓜迪亚机场停机坪上的飞机。 将这一层与清晰的建筑轮廓相结合，形成了一幅充满活力的地图，其中包含了这个令人着迷的公共数据中捕获的物理城市的许多元素。

👨🏻‍💻

Nerdbox 对于这篇文章，我从纽约市精彩而广泛的开放数据门户下载了 shapefile 和 DSM 文件。 我将它们加载到 QGIS 中，并将建筑占地面积形状文件与 DSM 文件结合起来，并应用“单带伪彩色”色标来可视化高程。

🙏🏻
